blocks.text: If z is a function of x and y, i.e., z = f(x, y), the total differential dz is obtained from the partial differentials dx and dy by the use of the following formula:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: This formula shows how the change in z is related to the changes in x and y.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of a surface, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like z, x, and y.

blocks.description: The figure illustrates the relationship between points on the surface and the changes in coordinates x, y, and z.

blocks.latex: \Delta z = \Delta x \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} + \Delta y \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}
blocks.description: The relationship describes how the total change in z (denoted by \Delta z) is affected by the changes in x and y.

blocks.text: Let P be a point (x, y) on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P on the surface. The change of position is limited by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21). The change in z due to the position from P to Q on the surface (y being kept constant).
